coredata.dc:title: The effect of electric field microenvironment on the exosome secretion of PC12 cells by chitosan carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhuojuan
Wang, Huajun
Zhou, Lin
Chen, Chengzhi
Zheng, Xiaofei
Zhou, Changren
coredata.dc:description: When neurons are sufficiently stimulated, they generate electrical signals with rapid voltage changes, and information is transferred between neurons through neurites. Chitosan carbon dots (CS CDs) possess unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, and superior electron transfer capabilities, making them promising materials for nerve tissue repair and replacement. Studies have shown that exosome (EXO) secretion by PC12 cells and neurite formation are critical for neural tissue repair. However, the effects of CDs on EXO secretion and neurite formation in an electric field microenvironment remain poorly explored. This study utilized CS to synthesize CDs and examined their impact on EXO secretion by PC12 cells in an electric field microenvironment. Findings demonstrated that CS-citric acid (CA) CDs had an average particle size of 4.5 nm with a lattice spacing of 0.248 nm and primarily consisted of graphitized carbon nuclei, hydroxyl, and amino groups. CS-CA CDs played a critical role in facilitating neurite elongation, especially with an electric field intensity of about 50 V/m. The combination of CS-CA CDs and electric field enhanced GAP43 expression in EXO secreted by PC12 cells. These results suggest that CS CDs have great potential for nerve injury treatment, repair, and regeneration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electric field
Chitosan carbon dots
Exosome

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PC12 cell culture, viability, electric field, and gene expression analysis
Effect of CS-CA CDs on the activity and differentiation of PC12 cells
Effect of CS-CA CDs on nerve growth of PC12 cells in electric field microenvironment
Effects of CS-CA CDs and electric field on EXO secretion and GAP43 gene expression in PC12 cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the Key Construction Aca-demic Subject for Scientifc Research Quality lmprovement of Guangdong Province (2021ZDJS139), Science and Technology Projects in Guangzhou (202206010158), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFE0206200), Science and technology development fund (FDCT) of Macao (FDCT 0009/2021/AMMJ, FDCT 0032/2022/AGJ), Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Plan (2023A0505020008), the Clinical Frontier Technology Program of the First Affiliated Hospital of Jinan University, China (JNU1AF-CFTP-2022-a01204).

This study was funded by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Fund Regional Joint Youth Fund ( 2019A515110262 ), Science and Technology Projects in Guangzhou (202206010158 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2022YFE0206200 ), Science and technology development fund (FDCT) of Macao ( FDCT 0009/2021/AMMJ , FDCT 0032/2022/AGJ ), Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Plan ( 2023A0505020008 ), the Clinical Frontier Technology Program of the First Affiliated Hospital of Jinan University, China ( JNU1AF-CFTP-2022-a01204 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The HRTEM image and particle size distribution of CS-CA CDs; (b) lattice spacing of CS-CA CDs; (c) XRD pattern of CS and CS-CA CDs; (d) FTIR spectra of CS and CS-CA CDs.
(a)XPS survey spectrum, (b)high-resolution C1s spectrum, (c)high-resolution N1s spectrum, and (d)high-resolution O1s spectrum of CS-CA CDs.

(a) UV–Vis spectrum, (b) fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, (c) excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra, and (d) excitation-dependent fluorescence emission normalized spectra of CS-CA CDs.

(a) Live/dead analysis of CS, CS CDs, and CS-CA CDs on PC12 cells at 1, 3, and 5 days (b) CS, CS CDs, and CS-CA CDs act on PC12 cells in percentage of cell viability at 1, 3, and 5 days; (c) CLSM images of CS, CS CDs, and CS-CA CDs acting on PC12 cells at day 1, 3, and 5 days.
(a) Photomicrographs of CS, CS CDs, CS-CA CDs, and PC12 cells co-cultured in an electric field microenvironment of 0, 20, 50, and 100 V/m for 24 h; (b) synaptic length of PC12 cells.

(a) Western Blot analysis of exosomes extracted from PC12 cells after co-culture of CS, CS-CA CDs in an electric field microenvironment of 0 and 50 V/m; (b) normalized histograms of relative expression levels of Calnexin, (c) CD63, and (d) CD9 protein.

(a) Real-time quantitative PCR amplification curve of GAP43 gene; (b) real-time fluorescence quantitative PCR meltdown curve of GAP43 gene; (c) comparison of the relative expression levels of GAP43 gene in EXO secreted by PC12 cells by CS and CS-CA CDs under 0 or 50 V/m electric field.

head.ce:title: The effect of electric field microenvironment on the exosome secretion of PC12 cells by chitosan carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Sports Medicine, The First Affiliated Hospital, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Speed Capability, The Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Precision Orthopedics and Regenerative Medicine, Jinan University, 510630 Guangzhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Speed Capability
The Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Precision Orthopedics and Regenerative Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Sports Medicine, The First Affiliated Hospital, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Speed Capability, The Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Precision Orthopedics and Regenerative Medicine, Jinan University, 510630, Guangzhou, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Zhuhai College of Science and Technology, Zhuhai 519040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhuhai College of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science, Zhuhai College of Science and Technology, Zhuhai 519040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: When neurons are sufficiently stimulated, they generate electrical signals with rapid voltage changes, and information is transferred between neurons through neurites. Chitosan carbon dots (CS CDs) possess unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, and superior electron transfer capabilities, making them promising materials for nerve tissue repair and replacement. Studies have shown that exosome (EXO) secretion by PC12 cells and neurite formation are critical for neural tissue repair. However, the effects of CDs on EXO secretion and neurite formation in an electric field microenvironment remain poorly explored. This study utilized CS to synthesize CDs and examined their impact on EXO secretion by PC12 cells in an electric field microenvironment. Findings demonstrated that CS-citric acid (CA) CDs had an average particle size of 4.5 nm with a lattice spacing of 0.248 nm and primarily consisted of graphitized carbon nuclei, hydroxyl, and amino groups. CS-CA CDs played a critical role in facilitating neurite elongation, especially with an electric field intensity of about 50 V/m. The combination of CS-CA CDs and electric field enhanced GAP43 expression in EXO secreted by PC12 cells. These results suggest that CS CDs have great potential for nerve injury treatment, repair, and regeneration.

ce:section.ce:para: CS is a naturally positively charged polysaccharide derived from chitin that exhibits excellent biocompatibility and degradability [1–3]. CS supports the growth of nerve and Schwann cells, inhibits fibroblast proliferation, and facilitates nerve cell axon elongation [4,5]. CA is a naturally occurring weak organic acid found in citrus fruits. In the biomedical field, CA is essential for nerve function [6]. Citrate metabolism plays a key role in driving de novo fatty acid synthesis, which is essential for Schwann cell myelination [7]. It was found that exogenous citrate may play a significant role in nerve regeneration by increasing the cytosolic acetyl-CoA pool in nerve fibers, thereby increasing neurite outgrowth and axonal transport [8].

CDs are polymer/carbon hybrid nanostructures comprising functional groups, polymer chains, and carbon nuclei [9]. CDs possess unique optical and chemical properties, high electron transfer efficiency, abundant structural defects, strong metal coordination, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and certain electrical conductivity [10–14]. Their electron transfer efficiency stems from conjugated carbon nuclei, functional group abundance, and heteroatom doping [15]. CA provides abundant chemically active sites, which can be combined with other molecules such as CS to achieve functional modifications [16]. CA-CDs as an undoped nanase, has demonstrated excellent cysteine oxidase mimicking activity and have been successfully used for sensitive detection of cysteine in serum samples [17]. CDs synthesized hydrothermally with CA and FAM-labeled DNA serve as fluorescent probes for detecting miRNA-91 in breast cancer via electrostatic adhesion [18]. Carboxymethyl CS CDs demonstrate exceptional biocompatibility, efficient endocytosis, and high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting Zn2+ in prostate cancer cells [19]. CDs derived from gum tragacanth and CS exhibit remarkable solubility, light stability, and enhanced cellular uptake due to surface hydrophilic groups [20]. A biocompatible CS CDs nanogel integrating pH-sensitive CS and fluorescent CDs offers potential for synergistic photothermal therapy [21]. Synthetic CDs enable ultra-sensitive detection of dopamine in plasma and PC12 cells, highlighting their suitability for neurotransmitter analysis [22]. It was found that manipulating the doping of CDs can control the branching patterns and growth of neuronal development [23].

EXO can carry various bioactive molecules, including neurotrophic factors, antioxidant enzymes, and miRNA, playing a critical role in the repair and regeneration processes following nerve injury [24]. Through EXO-mediated intercellular communication, damaged nerve cells receive supportive signals from healthy cells, increasing their survival and functional recovery [25–27]. EXO can carry miRNA and long non-coding RNA, which regulate gene expression and contribute to the growth, differentiation, and repair of nerve cells [28]. For instance, miR-1246 and miR-21a are effective in promoting neural differentiation and reducing glial scarring [29]. EXO are released by various cell types, including stem cells, with stem cell-derived EXO exhibiting enhanced regenerative potential due to their cargo of bioactive molecules, which more effectively support nerve cell repair and regeneration [30]. Research has utilized adipose-derived stem cell EXO to develop bionic neural guide catheters, demonstrating significant improvements in peripheral nerve regeneration in a rat sciatic nerve injury model, with better nerve function recovery and reduced tissue damage [27]. EXO also serve as natural nanocarriers for drugs and gene therapy molecules, enabling targeted delivery and minimizing side effects [31].

Electric fields, as non-invasive physical stimuli, significantly influence cellular behavior and function [32]. Within an electric field microenvironment, cell morphology, migration, proliferation, and differentiation are regulated [33]. Electric fields affect gene expression and protein synthesis by modulating the cell membrane potential, ion channels, and signal transduction pathways, ultimately impacting cell secretions [34–36]. In this microenvironment, electric fields alter the quantity and composition of EXO, thereby affecting their function in nerve cells [37]. The secretion process of EXO is modulated by electric fields [38], which can enhance EXO formation and release by influencing the membrane potential and intracellular calcium ion levels [39]. Moreover, the intensity and frequency of electric fields can be adjusted to modify the protein and RNA contents of EXO, improving their bioactivity [34]. However, no studies have reported the application of CS CDs in neural tissue repair.

This research focuses on developing innovative biomaterials for nerve tissue repair by leveraging the conductive properties of CDs for innovative applications in nerve repair. In this study, CS was selected as the precursor for CDs synthesis due to its favorable properties, including good biocompatibility, degradability, abundant amino groups, and the ability to be chemically modified to produce CS-CA CDs with excellent electrical conductivity and stability. The study involved characterizing the physicochemical properties of CS-CA CDs, analyzing their influence on neural cell behavior, and examining the effects of CS-CA CDs on PC12 cells and their EXO secretion under an electric field microenvironment.

CS (M.W ∼ 193,400 Da, degree of deacetylation 85 %, viscosity 200–800 cP), CA, and quinine sulfate were procured from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PC12 cells were supplied by Xinyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Ultrapure water with 18.2 MΩ resistance, prepared using the Milli-Q system (Jingwei, China), was used throughout the experiments. All other chemicals and reagents employed in the study were of analytical grade. The selection of this specification of CS was based on its good solubility, surface functionalization, and moderate viscosity, which contributed to stable synthesis results during the CDs synthesis process.

To synthesize CDs, 1 g of CS was dispersed in ultrapure water and transferred into a hydrothermal reactor lined with a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene container. For CS-CA CDs, 1 g of CS was dispersed in ultrapure water, followed by the gradual addition of CA at a mass ratio of 1:1. The mixture was stirred until homogeneous and then transferred into the reactor. The reaction for both methods was conducted at 200 °C for 10 h. After the hydrothermal process, the reactor was allowed to cool to room temperature. The reaction mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed using a 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off membrane for 48 h. The dialyzed solution was then freeze-dried, and the resultant CDs were ground into a fine powder, sealed, and stored in a dry environment for further use.

The chemical structure of CDs was analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (IRAffinity-1, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with an XRD diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Kyoto, Japan) to determine crystalline structure. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution at various excitation wavelengths were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra, ranging from 200 to 800 nm, were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The morphology of CDs was observed with a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Kyoto, Japan). The elemental composition, content, and bonding configurations of the CDs were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher, Massachusetts, USA). Additional observations were performed with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Olympus FV1200, Olympus Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) and a fluorescence microscope (Olympus BX51, Olympus Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). Electrical conductivity of the samples was measured with the conductivity meter (DDSJ-308 A, Precision Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China). The zeta potential of the samples was determined by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Nano ZS 90, Malvern instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK). Disposable folded capillary cells (DTS1070) were pre-rinsed with pure ethanol and MilliQ water before use. The sample was injected into folded capillary cells and placed in the Zetasizer at 20 °C for 120 s. The sample should be injected slowly and analyzed only when there are no visible bubbles. Finally, the Zeta potential was calculated by Smoluhovsky equation.

Details of the electrical stimulation device setup are provided in the Supplementary Materials. The design of the device diagram was based on the previously described methodology [40]. Briefly, the experimental configuration, illustrated in Fig. S1, comprised three interconnected culture dishes. The central dish, measuring 9 mm in diameter, was designated for PC12 cell culture, while the left and right dishes (6 mm in diameter each) contained DMEM. The bottom of the central dish was lined with two 30 × 30 × 0.2 mm glass slides separated by a 4 mm gap and secured with 3140 silicone adhesive (3140 RTV, Dow Corning, Korea). A rectangular area formed by four glass pieces (2 × 1 mm2 and 1 × 1 mm2) served as the cell culture region. Fluid continuity across dishes was maintained via salt bridges composed of 1 % sterile agar mixed with DMEM culture medium. These bridges were immersed in the culture medium, creating a closed circuit. Silver wires were inserted into the culture dish edges connected to the salt bridges and transmitted current for electrical stimulation. Red and black wires, representing positive and negative electrodes, respectively, connected the culture setup to electronic equipment that monitored and controlled the electrical stimulation intensity. The central dish facilitated sample addition and served as the primary site for the cell culture and stimulation experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PC12 cell culture, viability, electric field, and gene expression analysis
ce:section.ce:para: PC12 cells, derived from rat pheochromocytoma, were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % heat-inactivated horse serum and 5 % fetal bovine serum. The cells were maintained at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. The culture medium was replaced every 2–3 days to sustain optimal growth conditions. Subculturing was performed upon reaching about 80 % confluency. Cells were detached using either a cell scraper or brief trypsinization, neutralized with a serum-containing medium, and re-plated at appropriate densities to ensure consistent proliferation and viability. All PC12 cells were cultured with 50 ng/mL nerve growth factor (NGF).

The viability of PC12 cells treated with CDs was assessed using a CCK-8 assay and live/dead cell staining. Cells were seeded in 96-well plates and treated with CDs for 1, 3, and 5 days. The CCK-8 solution was added to the wells, and absorbance at 450 nm was measured to determine cell viability. For live/dead staining, Calcein-AM (green fluorescence for live cells) and propidium iodide (PI, red fluorescence for dead cells) were used, and the cells were visualized under a fluorescence microscope. Additionally, cells cultured on collagen-coated confocal dishes were fixed and stained with PI to observe cell viability and morphology under a confocal microscope. Detailed experimental procedures are provided in the Supplementary Materials.

A 6-well plate was prepared by adding 1 mL of 5 μg/mL rat tail collagen type I solution, which was pre-dissolved in 0.006 mol/L HAc, to each well. The plate was left uncovered to dry overnight on a super-clean table. The collagen-coated wells were washed 3–4 times with PBS to remove residual acid. Cells were detached and digested with cold PBS, re-suspended in complete media, and seeded into the 6-well plate at a density of 5000 cells/mL per well. The cells were cultured in a 37 °C incubator with 5 % CO2 for 24 h to allow for adhesion to the well surface. After discarding the culture medium and rinsing it with PBS, the cells were further cultured for an additional 24 h. The treated cells were subjected to electric fields of 0 V/m, 20 V/m, 50 V/m, and 100 V/m using a custom-designed parallel plate capacitor setup, ensuring a uniform electric field across the wells. At the end of the culture period, the medium was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS three times. The cells were fixed with 1 % paraformaldehyde solution for 10 min, followed by three PBS washes. A permeabilization treatment was applied for 10 min, after which the cells were washed again three times with PBS. Rhodamine B-labeled cyclic peptide was added to each well for 60 min to stain the cell structures, followed by PBS rinses. DAPI stain was applied to label cell nuclei, and after 10–30 min, glass coverslips were mounted. The cells were observed under a microscope, where the DAPI-labeled nuclei appeared blue and the cyclic peptide-stained structures appeared red. Images were captured for analysis.

The EXO extraction procedures are described in the Supplementary Materials. Briefly, cells treated with or without CDs were cultured in an electric field microenvironment of 0 V/m or 50 V/m for 24 h. After exposure, the cell culture supernatants were collected, and EXO were isolated through differential ultracentrifugation to evaluate the effects of CDs and electric fields on EXO secretion.

For RNA extraction from EXO, cells exposed to electric fields of 0 V/m and 100 V/m were utilized. Using a 90 mm petri dish, 5 mL of 5 μg/mL rat tail collagen type I (dissolved in 0.006 mol/L HAc) was added to the dish and left uncovered to dry overnight in a super-clean table. The coated dishes were washed 3–4 times with PBS. Cells were detached with cold PBS, re-suspended in a complete medium, and seeded at a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL per dish. They were cultured at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator with 5 % CO2 for 24 h to allow attachment. The medium was discarded, cells were rinsed with PBS, and further cultured for another 24 h with conditions and electrical stimulation parameters detailed in the experimental protocol. During this time, serum-free 1640 base medium was used for all groups. Following the culture period, at least 50 mL of supernatant from each group was collected. EXO was extracted from the culture supernatant using sequential low-speed centrifugation to remove debris, filtration, high-speed centrifugation, and ultracentrifugation. Protein samples from the isolated EXO were analyzed by Western blot. Cells collected from the dishes were subjected to RNA extraction using Trizol, and the RNA samples were tested using PCR. Detailed methods for these procedures are provided in the Supplementary Materials.

The quantum yield (Q) of CDs was calculated by the subsequent equation:
(1)
Q =
Q R
I
I R
A R
A
n 2
n R 2
where Q represents the quantum yield of CDs, I denotes the integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and R refers to the reference fluorophore with a known quantum yield. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield of 0.54 at 360 nm) was utilized as the reference standard.

Fig. 1a and b display the HRTEM images of CS-CA CDs. The CS-CA CDs exhibited spherical morphology with relatively uniform particle sizes and good dispersion. The particle size distribution ranged from 2 to 7 nm. Based on fitted data, the average particle size of the CS-CA CDs was determined to be 4.5 nm. The lattice spacing was measured at 0.248 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal face of graphitic carbon. This confirms that CS-CA CDs possess a well-defined lattice structure [41]. Fig. 1c presents the XRD patterns of CS and CS-CA CDs. The principal crystalline peak of CS appeared at about 20°, while that of the CS-CA CDs was observed at 24°, with a broader peak. This observation suggests that the crystallinity of CS decreased following hydrothermal carbonization, resulting in the formation of amorphous carbon and smaller particles. The hydrothermal carbonization process disrupted the ordered structure and increased the amorphous carbon content [42]. Additionally, the presence of graphitized carbon nuclei in CS-CA CDs was confirmed, consistent with the TEM results. To further investigate the surface functional groups in CS-CA CDs, FTIR characterization was conducted for CS and CS-CA CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, the characteristic peaks of CS at about 3335, 2904, 1668, 1573, 1386, and 1076 cm−1 were assigned to the functional groups -NH/-OH, -CH3/-CH2, C=C/C=N, CN, C-OH, and C-O-C, respectively [43–45]. In comparison with CS, the -NH/-OH stretching vibrations in CS-CA CDs were decreased, while amino and C-OH absorptions were increased. These changes may be attributed to the degradation of CS chains and the dehydrating decomposition of pyranose rings [46]. The presence of CO, NH, and C-OH groups in both CS and CS-CA CDs indicates that the chemical structure of CS was partially retained after carbonization, alongside the formation of new graphitized or aromatic structures in the CDs.

Fig. 2 presents the XPS spectrum of CS-CA CDs, highlighting characteristic peaks for C1s, N1s, and O1s, confirming the incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups. The elemental composition of CS-CA CDs was determined to be 63.5 % C, 4.7 % N, and 31.8 % O, with levels of C, N, and O being 1.0, 0.6, and 1.1 times higher, respectively, than those of CS. In the C1s spectrum of CS, peaks were observed at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, CN, and CO groups, respectively indicating the formation of graphitized carbon nuclei [47]. The N1s spectrum revealed peaks at 399.9 and 401.7 eV, attributed to amino N and graphite N, while the O1s spectrum exhibited peaks at 531.9 and 532.4 eV, associated with C-OH and C-O-C groups [48]. These results from the XPS analysis confirmed that the primary components of CS-CA CDs included graphitized carbon nuclei, hydroxyl groups, and amino groups.

Fig. 3a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CS-CA CDs, with a peak centered at 288 nm, attributed to the n-π* electron transition [49,50]. This feature reflects the CC conjugate structure and the presence of surface defect states. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CS-CA CDs are shown in Fig. 3b, where an excitation wavelength of 372 nm produces a maximum emission peak at 460 nm, corresponding to blue fluorescence emission. Under ultraviolet light, this results in visible blue fluorescence. Fig. 3c shows the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the CS-CA CDs. Variations in the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm influenced both the position and intensity of the emission peak. Initially, the emission intensity increased and then declined, with the peak position redshifting as the excitation wavelength increased. These excitation-dependent characteristics are attributed to the conjugated carbon nucleus structure and radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs [51].

Fig. 3d shows the normalized fluorescence emission spectra of CS-CA CDs, demonstrating a redshift in the emission wavelength as the excitation wavelength increased, indicating that the fluorescence emission was excitation-dependent. While the emission wavelength shifted with changes in the excitation wavelength, the shape of the emission spectra remained relatively constant. This behavior is attributed to the varying sizes of the nanoparticles within the sample and the presence of diverse emission traps on the surface of the CDs [52]. The multiple functional groups (C, N, and O) present on the surface of CS-CA CDs generate a variety of surface states with different energy levels, forming a range of emission traps. These emission traps dominate fluorescence emission at specific wavelengths, resulting in excitation-dependent fluorescence properties [53]. Using quinine sulfate as a reference standard, the quantum yield of CS-CA CDs was measured to be 14 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CS-CA CDs on the activity and differentiation of PC12 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of CS-CA CDs on PC12 cell behavior was examined, with CS CDs included for comparative analysis. The characterization data for CS CDs are presented in Figs. S2–S5. As shown in Fig. 4a, FDA/PI staining was employed to assess the viability, proliferation, and elongation of PC12 cells. The findings revealed that most PC12 cells remained viable on the first day of culture, indicating the minimal impact of CS-CA CDs on cell viability. After three days, cells across all groups exhibited high activity and were aligned in the axon direction. By the fifth day, a well-defined cell-cell contact network was observed, supporting functional expression in cells. Fig. 4b further confirms that CS-CA CDs demonstrate excellent biocompatibility with PC12 cells, highlighting their potential for biological applications. After 1, 3, and 5 days of co-culture with the blank control group, no adverse effects on the normal growth or proliferation of PC12 cells were observed, with toxicity levels to PC12 cells measured as 0 or 1. Fig. 4c presents CLSM images of PC12 cell differentiation with CS-CA CDs and CS CDs at 1, 3, and 5 days. Both CS-CA CDs and CS CDs exhibited prominent blue fluorescence, and the cells displayed healthy morphology with no apparent signs of apoptosis or necrosis during the observation period. Notably, CS-CA CDs demonstrated more intense blue fluorescence compared to CS CDs while maintaining excellent biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CS-CA CDs on nerve growth of PC12 cells in electric field microenvironment
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5a and b illustrate the influence of CS, CS CDs, and CS-CA CDs on PC12 cell growth under electric field microenvironments with intensities of 0, 20, 50, and 100 V/m. Neurite elongation increased significantly with higher electric field intensity, particularly in groups treated with CS-CA CDs and CS CDs. However, no further enhancement in neurite growth was observed when the electric field strength increased from 50 V to 100 V, suggesting that about 50 V represents an optimal voltage for promoting neurite elongation. Furthermore, the presence of CDs, especially those containing CA, markedly enhanced neurite growth. Across all voltage levels, the CS-CA CDs group achieved the greatest neurite length, emphasizing the pivotal role of CS-CA CDs in facilitating neurite elongation under an electric field. Additionally, the neurites were oriented towards the cathode within the electric field microenvironment, with stronger field intensities resulting in more pronounced alignment towards the cathode. This indicates that the electric field effectively directs neurite orientation.

The incorporation of CA into CS-CA CDs enhanced the stability and conductivity of the system, creating favorable conditions for the elongation of PC12 cells under electric field stimulation. The interaction process of CS and CA is shown in Fig. S6. The zeta potential analysis, shown in Fig. S7, revealed that CS-CA CDs (−14.6 mV) had a greater negative charge compared to CS CDs (−12.0 mV). This increase in negative charge, attributed to the carboxyl groups provided by CA, improved the electrostatic repulsion between particles, enhancing stability by reducing aggregation and enabling a more uniform distribution in the solution. This stability supported a higher concentration of free ions [54]. Additionally, the electrical conductivity of CS-CA CDs was eight times higher than that of CS CDs, reflecting a substantial reduction in system resistance. According to the relationship between conductivity and resistance, enhanced conductivity indicates reduced resistance, allowing a greater current to pass through the system at the same voltage, as per Ohm's law [55,56]. This increase in free ion concentration contributed to better performance in biostimulation and electrochemical applications. The addition of CA not only enhanced the electrical properties of the system but also facilitated a more uniform electric field distribution. This uniformity minimized inefficiencies caused by regions with excessive or insufficient electric field strength [57], thereby improving the overall electrical environment. These improvements significantly supported neurite elongation under optimal electric field conditions. Although the charge played an important role in the dispersion, stability, and behavior of the CDs in the electric field, the effects of the CDs on PC12 cells were not solely dependent on the charge. Differences in the surface functional groups of CDs also directly affected the biocompatibility and cellular reactions. More amino and hydroxyl functional groups were on the surface of CS-CA CDs, which could enhance the interaction with cells and promote nerve differentiation and growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CS-CA CDs and electric field on EXO secretion and GAP43 gene expression in PC12 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6a presents the Western blot analysis of Calnexin, CD63, and CD9 protein expression levels in EXO secreted by PC12 cells under varying electric field microenvironments. The histograms in Fig. 6b-d show the relative expression levels of these target proteins normalized to β-actin under different experimental conditions. CD63 and CD9, specific markers of exosomal membrane proteins, were markedly expressed across all experimental groups, with especially high expression observed in groups of CS and CS-CA CDs at 50 V. The clear and strong bands indicated the presence of EXO in the samples. The addition of CS-CA CDs significantly increased the expression levels of exosomal markers CD63, CD9, and Calnexin, suggesting that CS-CA CDs facilitated EXO secretion and induced endoplasmic reticulum stress. The application of a 50 V/m electric field further amplified the effects of CS-CA CDs on the promotion of EXO markers and Calnexin expression. This enhancement was most pronounced in the group of CS-CA CDs at 50 V. These findings demonstrate that the combination of CS-CA CDs and the electric field exerted a synergistic effect, significantly boosting EXO formation and secretion.

The fluorescence quantitative PCR amplification curve (Fig. 7a) demonstrated that amplification for all samples commenced between 28 and 32 cycles, confirming the successful detection of the GAP43 gene and indicating high PCR amplification efficiency. Fig. 7b reveals a peak at about 85 °C, indicative of high specificity in GAP43 gene amplification without nonspecific products or primer dimers. These results confirmed that the primary product amplified by PCR was GAP43. The relative expression levels of the GAP43 gene, associated with nerve growth-related proteins in EXO secreted by PC12 cells, are shown in Fig. 7c. Under field intensities of 0 V/m and 50 V/m, CS-CA CDs significantly enhanced GAP43 expression. The increased expression under varying electric fields highlights a robust synergistic effect between CS-CA CDs and the applied electric field. GAP43 is a neuron-specific protein critical for axonal growth, neuroplasticity, and nerve repair, serving as a central indicator of nerve regeneration and growth processes [58,59]. The marked increase in GAP43 expression induced by CS-CA CDs suggested that these nanoparticles possessed significant biological activity in promoting neural growth and repair. This activity may stem from enhanced EXO secretion or activation of intracellular signaling pathways facilitated by CS-CA CDs. The electric field appears to amplify these effects, likely by increasing cell membrane permeability, promoting EXO release, or activating pathways linked to nerve growth and regeneration [37,57]. The elevated GAP43 expression observed indicates enhanced neural repair and regeneration mechanisms. High levels of GAP43 are associated with active axonal remodeling and synaptic plasticity, essential for repairing damaged nerves and forming new neural connections [60,61].

Chemical structure analysis of CS-CA CDs revealed the presence of CO, NH, and C-OH groups, indicating partial preservation of the CS structure after carbonization, along with the formation of new graphitized or aromatic structures. The fluorescence properties of CS-CA CDs were excitation-dependent, and they exhibited strong blue fluorescence while maintaining excellent biocompatibility with PC12 cells. The addition of CDs, particularly those containing CA, significantly enhanced neurite growth, with the electric field intensifying neurite alignment towards the cathode, demonstrating the potential for directed nerve growth. Furthermore, CS-CA CDs significantly promoted the expression of GAP43 in EXO secreted by PC12 cells under a 50 V/m electric field. Enhanced GAP43 expression, associated with axonal growth and neuroplasticity, underscores the role of CS-CA CDs in improving nerve repair mechanisms. These findings highlight the potential of CS-CA CDs, especially in combination with electric fields, for therapeutic applications in nerve damage repair and regeneration. CS-CA CDs can be incorporated into scaffolds or hydrogels for targeted nerve regeneration treatments. Furthermore, their strong fluorescence properties and surface functionalization with nitrogen and oxygen groups can allow for bioimaging applications or serve as drug delivery vehicles that are more effectively targeted to nerve cells. This study focused primarily on in vitro experiments using PC12 cells but may not fully represent the complex in vivo environment. Future research will focus on animal models to evaluate the biocompatibility, toxicity, and long-term efficacy of CS-CA CDs in nerve repair. While enhanced nerve growth appears to be linked to increased exosome secretion and intracellular pathway activation, the precise molecular mechanisms involved remain unclear.

Zhuojuan Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Huajun Wang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Lin Zhou: Software, Formal analysis. Chengzhi Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaofei Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Changren Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the Key Construction Aca-demic Subject for Scientifc Research Quality lmprovement of Guangdong Province (2021ZDJS139), Science and Technology Projects in Guangzhou (202206010158), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFE0206200), Science and technology development fund (FDCT) of Macao (FDCT 0009/2021/AMMJ, FDCT 0032/2022/AGJ), Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Plan (2023A0505020008), the Clinical Frontier Technology Program of the First Affiliated Hospital of Jinan University, China (JNU1AF-CFTP-2022-a01204).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.142747.
